coredata.dc:title: Tailoring the sensing abilities of carbon nanodots obtained from olive solid wastes
coredata.dc:creator: Sawalha, Shadi
Silvestri, Alessandro
Criado, Alejandro
Bettini, Simona
Prato, Maurizio
Valli, Ludovico
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots are emerging in the nanotechnology field as the newest class of fluorescent probes, owing to their high water-solubility, good chemical and photo-stability and tunable photoluminescence. The ability of metal cations to quench carbon dots fluorescence is already well known. Anyway, a dedicated study directed to the rational design of the optimal carbon dot probe is still missing. Herein we present a novel synthetic method for the production of carbon nanodots from an economic and environmental polluting starting material: olive solid wastes. The obtained carbon dots are efficiently quenched in presence of Fe3+, allowing the cation detection. Starting from a comprehensive study on their quenching mechanism, we have been able to develop effective strategies to modulate the sensing properties of these carbon dots. The sensitivity and linear range of the probe have been tuned by modulating the surface functionalization of the nanoparticles. The selectivity toward Fe3+ has been improved by growing around the dots a porous microsphere of ion imprinted polymer. The results highlighted in this work demonstrate that the carbon dots sensing properties can be modulated to meet the requirement of the users, allowing the design of tailored probes for a wide range of applications.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic procedure for the production of: a) CNDs, b) CNDs-EDA, c) CNDs-Dopa.

Morphological characterization of CNDs obtained from olive solid wastes: a) TEM micrograph, b) TEM size distribution histogram, c) representative HR-TEM micrograph, d) AFM height micrograph, e) AFM height profile and f) AFM height distribution histogram. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

CND compositional analysis: a) XPS survey spectrum, b) high resolution C1s core, c) high resolution O1s core and d) high resolution N1s core, e) FT-IR spectrum f) table with FT-IR peaks assignments. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Optical properties of CNDs: a) UV–Vis absorption, b) PL intensity at different excitation wavelengths, c) pH fluorescence dependence, d) and photo-stability at 360 nm irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Ion sensing with CNDs; a) selectivity to different cations, b) ion interfering of different cations with Fe3+, c) fluorescence spectra showing the quenching effect at different [Fe3+], d) quenching efficiency and calibration curve for Fe3+ in Ultrapure water, e) calibration curve for Fe3+ in tap-water.
Quenching Mechanism of CNDs with Fe3+; a) Stern Volmer correlation, b) fluorescence life time, c) FTIR spectra, d) and UV–Vis absorption. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Tuning of the CND sensing properties based on the surface modification. a) Quenching efficiency and calibration curve of CNDs-EDA for different [Fe3+] in ultrapure water. b) Quenching efficiency and calibration curve of CNDs-Dopa for different [Fe3+] in ultrapure water. C) Comparison between CNDs, CNDs-EDA, CNDs-Dopa quenching efficiencies. d) Table comparing the sensing parameters of the three CNDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
IIP and NIP synthesis; a) schematic representation of CND-IIP synthetic procedure, b) TEM micrograph of IIP and c) TEM micrograph of NIP. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Ion sensing with CND-IIP; a) PL relative intensity of IIP and NIP, b) quenching efficiency vs. [Fe3+] for IIP and NIP, c) IIP selectivity for different cations ([Mx+] = 25 μM). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots are emerging in the nanotechnology field as the newest class of fluorescent probes, owing to their high water-solubility, good chemical and photo-stability and tunable photoluminescence. The ability of metal cations to quench carbon dots fluorescence is already well known. Anyway, a dedicated study directed to the rational design of the optimal carbon dot probe is still missing. Herein we present a novel synthetic method for the production of carbon nanodots from an economic and environmental polluting starting material: olive solid wastes. The obtained carbon dots are efficiently quenched in presence of Fe3+, allowing the cation detection. Starting from a comprehensive study on their quenching mechanism, we have been able to develop effective strategies to modulate the sensing properties of these carbon dots. The sensitivity and linear range of the probe have been tuned by modulating the surface functionalization of the nanoparticles. The selectivity toward Fe3+ has been improved by growing around the dots a porous microsphere of ion imprinted polymer. The results highlighted in this work demonstrate that the carbon dots sensing properties can be modulated to meet the requirement of the users, allowing the design of tailored probes for a wide range of applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon nanodots (CNDs) are novel fluorescent, quasi-spherical carbon particles with size below 10 nm [1]. In the past decade CNDs have gained interest due to their unique optical properties, good biocompatibility, low toxicity, great aqueous stability and facile synthesis [2,3]. CNDs own several unique chemical and physical properties which are dictated by their carbogenic core, elemental composition and surface chemistry [4–6]. In particular, CND surface represents a vast part of the nanoparticle which possesses a huge area/volume ratio. The surface is the part of the particle directly in contact with the surrounding environment, dictating their behavior and interaction with other species in proximity. For these reasons is fundamental to properly modulate the CND surface to obtain the best performances in the selected applications.

Thanks to their peculiar properties, CNDs are studied for several applications in biomedicine, optoelectronics, catalysis and chemical sensing [7,8]. In this last field, basing on fluorescence quenching or turn on, CNDs could serve as sensors for a wide range of analytes such as cations, anions, small molecules, macromolecules, cells and bacteria [9]. Mostly, CNDs are used for the detection of metal ions such as Cu2+ [10], Hg2+ [11], Ag+ [12], Cr6+ [13] and Fe3+ [14]. In particular, Fe3+ sensing is of great importance for monitoring its levels in water basins and biological systems, since its deficiency or overloading are tightly associated with many environmental concerns and human diseases [15]. In fact, Fe is the most abundant metal in biological systems, playing a pivotal role in many biochemical processes, such as oxygen transport through heme, cellular metabolism and cofactor for enzymatic reactions [16]. Both the deficiency and overload of Fe can cause serious medical and biological disorders. For example, Fe3+ deﬁciency is correlated to anemia disease [17], while its overloading a strong risk factor for type 2 diabetes [18], Hepatitis C [19], Parkinson’s [20] and Alzheimer’s diseases [21].

Several synthetic routes have been explored for the production of CNDs starting from a wide range of man-made and natural precursors [22–31]. A vast number of starting materials of natural origin were investigated, as food and beverages (eg. milk [32], coffee [33], tea [34], flour [34], potato [35] and fruit [36]), plants (eg. rose flowers [37], aloe [38], Jinhua bergamot [39], coriander [40] and lotus [41]) or animal derivatives (eg. egg [42], hairs [43], urines [44], silk [45] and wool [46]. Among these, wastes of industrial and agricultural processes are highly appealing both from an economical and environmental point of view, due to their low cost and to the chance to requalify the production side-products. Under this aspect an example of affordable precursor is olive solid waste (OSW). During the olive oil production, massive amounts of solids and liquids residues are generated. The management of such residues is a challenge both from the economic and environmental aspect. In fact, olive mill wastes are a significant source of environmental pollution, as they can modify the soil chemical and physical properties, such as porosity, acidity, salinity and heavy metal contents [47]. Additionally, they contain organic molecules with phytotoxic effects on plants and aquatic species; such as lipids, phosphates, tannins and phenols [48]. In particular the last two compounds have microbial growth inhibiting properties that can affect the microenvironment in the soil [48]. The problem of the olive wastes management is particularly relevant in the Mediterranean basin, where more than 98% of global olive production is concentrated and the olive oil market has been hugely expanding over the last two decades [49,50]. The solid fraction of OSW has little economic value, and only occasionally it is recycled in pomace oil: low-quality oil employed for industrial applications. Most commonly OSWs are burned for heating purposes, thus contributing to the atmospheric pollution [47].

Here, we suggest an alternative application of this abundant biomass, as low-cost starting material for the production of carbon nanodots (CNDs). In this study we report a novel synthetic method for CND production, consisting in pyrolysis of OSW followed by oxidation and extraction in oxidizing media. The synthesized CNDs allow the efficient Fe3+ sensing in water. The quenching efficiency, linear range, sensitivity, limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantification (LOQ) of the probe have been evaluated. Particular attention has been paid to the sensing mechanisms of these CNDs. Furthermore, CND surface has been modified introducing amino and catechol groups in the dots structure, to evaluate the effects of surface modification on the CND sensing properties against Fe3+. Finally, in order to improve the sensor selectivity to Fe3+ we have developed an ion imprinted polymer (IIP) using CNDs as transducer component.

Here, we suggest an alternative application of OSW, as low-cost starting material for the production of CNDs. We report a novel synthetic method consisting in the pyrolysis of OSWs at 600 °C for an hour [51] followed by an extraction and oxidation with H2O2 (Fig. 1 a). Hydrogen peroxide acts as a strong oxidizing agent helping in breaking down the carbonaceous particles present in OSW, producing CNDs rich of oxygenated groups on their surfaces [52]. After purification and lyophilization, CNDs are obtained with a production yield of 10%. The unreacted residual carbonated olive solid wastes (COSWs) can be recycled and used as a precursor for a new batch, minimizing the waste product of the reaction.

The synthetic strategy allows to modulate the surface chemistry of the dots by introducing ligands provided of terminal amino groups. To prove the versatility of the proposed synthesis and demonstrate the possibility to perform a one-pot surface functionalization we modified the surface of the dots with ethylenediamine (EDA) and dopamine (Dopa). We chose EDA and dopamine to dope the CNDs in order to modify their affinity for Fe3+, modulating in this way their sensing efficiency toward this cation. We expect that dopamine, well-known as Fe chelator, will increase the affinity and hence the sensitivity of the probe [53]. On the other side, the use of EDA is a well-known approach to produce nitrogen-enriched CNDs [54]. We expect that increase of nitrogen groups and the decrease in hydroxyl and carboxylic ones will reduce the affinity of the CNDs toward Fe3+. Carbon dots functionalized with EDA (CNDs-EDA) and with Dopa (CNDs-Dopa) have been synthetized using a weight ratio of 1:2 and 1:1 of COSWs: ligand during the oxidation step (Fig. 1 b, c). We believe that the CND nitrogen doping could occur, during the CND formation, through electrostatic adsorption of EDA on carboxylic groups or through nucleophilic attack of the amines on the epoxide rings. Due to the strong oxidative conditions, the formation of hydroxylamine, azo or nitro compounds cannot be excluded, triggering side reactions during the CND synthesis. Nevertheless, XPS analysis rules out the presence of such oxidized moieties in the purified CND structure. In fact, N-O bonds have a very diagnostic peak in N1s core of XPS spectrum at 405-402 eV, absent from both CNDs-EDA and CNDs-Dopa spectra [55]. The morphology of the CNDs obtained from OSW has been investigated by TEM and AFM (Fig. 2 ). The TEM micrographs show well dispersed nanoparticles with good contrast and narrow size dispersion (2.8 ± 0.6 nm) (Fig. 2a and b). Similar dimensions have been obtained by AFM for the height of the CNDs, demonstrating that the particles have quasi-spherical morphology (Fig. 2d, e, f). HR-TEM images show that the dots have a crystalline structure with inter-lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, distinctive of several reported CNDs (Fig. 2c) [56–62]. Analogously, the TEM micrographs of CNDs functionalized with EDA and Dopa shows well-formed nanoparticles with regular morphology and crystalline structure. CNDs-EDA present an average size dispersion of 2.5 ± 0.7 nm (Figs. S1a and b). CNDs-Dopa instead presents slight larger size than the original CNDs or CNDs-EDA: 3.2 ± 1 nm (Figs. S2a and b).

In parallel, the chemical composition of the CNDs has been investigated by XPS and FT-IR (Fig. 3 ). XPS survey spectrum shows three major peaks at 285, 400 and 532 eV, attributable to carbon, nitrogen and oxygen respectively (Fig. 3a). The reduced amount of N in the sample (3.9%) indicates that only a few amino groups (pyridinic and pyrrolic) are present in the CND surface. The elevated quantity of oxygen (37.6%) suggests that a large number of oxygenated groups have formed on the CND surface due to the oxidative action of H2O2.

Having a closer look to the high-resolution C 1s core (Fig. 3b), four peaks can be deconvoluted, attributable to: C-C (284.8 eV), C-O/C-N (286.2 eV) C=O (288.2eV) and O-C=O (289.2 eV) components [63,64]. The O1s spectrum exhibits two peaks at 531.8 and 533 eV, which can be assigned to O=C and HO-C/C-O-C, respectively (Fig. 3c) [3,65–67]. From XPS data, it results that the oxygenated groups are present in the CND structure as carbonyl, carboxylate, hydroxyl and epoxide groups. Such conclusion is supported as well from the FT-IR spectrum, where well-defined peaks are attributed to hydroxyl (1030, 3380 cm−1), epoxide (1242 cm−1) carboxylate (1397 cm−1) and carbonyl (1650 cm−1) groups (Fig. 3e and f) [68,69]. Finally, the presence of oxidized and acidic groups on the surface was confirmed by ζ-potentials measurement showing a strong negative charge of −32 eV (Table S1) [3,70,71]. Such negative potential value confers to the CNDs an excellent solubility in water [66].

The XPS analysis of the surface functionalized carbon dots, CNDs-EDA and CNDs-Dopa, show a remarkable increase in the nitrogen content of the materials, confirming the successful modification with the chosen ligands. In particular, CNDs-EDA presented an increase of nitrogen content up to 17.8 at% (Fig. S3a) and CNDs-Dopa up to 7.8% (Fig. S4a). The nitrogen content in CNDs-Dopa is higher than the bare CNDs but lower than CNDs-EDA, according with the fact that dopamine in its structure has half nitrogen atoms with respect to EDA. XPS of CNDs-Dopa give us a further proof that the functionalization succeeded: from the deconvolution of the C1s core, the component attributed to the aromatic carbon atoms increased in intensity (Fig. S4b). In parallel, in the O1s core the HO-C component is becoming dominant, according to the presence of catechol rings on the surface of the dots (Fig. S4c) [72].

These findings are supported as well by FT-IR spectra. In the FTIR spectrum of CNDs-EDA two new peaks appear at 1560, 1309 cm−1. These peaks can be attributed to C=N/N-H (bending) [73–75] and C(sp2)-N [76,77], confirming the doping of nitrogen and NH2 groups on CND-EDA surface (Fig. S3e). FT-IR spectrum of CNDs-Dopa shows the typical dopamine peaks (938, 1271 cm−1), which can be attributed to the presence of the catechol ring (Fig. S4e) [78–80]. The FT-IR spectrum, XPS survey and deconvoluted high resolution spectra of C1s, O1s and N1s for CNDs-EDA and CNDs-Dopa are shown and discussed in supporting information (Figs. S3 and S4).
In CNDs-EDA as result of the amine doping and surface modification, the ζ-potential of these dots increases to −10 mV (Table S1). CNDs-Dopa shows a neutral charge.

To explore the optical properties of the prepared CNDs, UV–visible and photoluminescence spectral acquisitions have been conducted. UV–visible spectrum of CNDs shows a strong absorption at 220–320 nm which can be ascribed to the π-π∗ and n-π∗ transition arising from aromatic C=C bonds (sp2 domain) and C=O groups respectively [3,81] (Fig. 4 a). Fig. S5a shows the absorption spectrum of CNDs-EDA. The characteristic absorption peaks in the ranges from 250 to 406 nm can be attributed to the amino groups on the surface of CNDs-EDA [77]. In particular, these peaks at 284 and 335 nm could be ascribed to the typical absorption for n-π∗ transition of N=O and/or C=O in sp3 hybrid regions [74]. Regarding CNDs-Dopa, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum indicates the presence of the dopamine catechol ring on the surface of the C-dots (Fig. S6a, peak at 280 nm) [82].

The CNDs were characterized by photoluminescence (PL) at different excitation wavelengths (from 300 to 480 nm). As described for most CNDs, the emission depends on the excitation wavelength, shifting from 440 to 540 nm with a maximum emission at 460 nm (Ex. 360 nm) (Fig. 4b) [73,83,84]. The CNDs quantum yield (QY) was calculated to be 3% using quinine sulfate (QY = 54%) as standard reference. The pH-dependence of the CND PL was investigated as shown in Fig. 4c. The PL is stable at the pH range from 4 to 11, which is beneficial for the fluorescence probe to be used in complex biological systems and in sensing applications [76]. Moreover, CNDs show a very good photo-stability. When continuously irradiated for 120 min at 360 nm they only loose the 6% of PL (Fig. 4d). A complete photo-luminescence characterization of the surface modified dots has been performed and reported in SI (Figs. S5 and S6).

The aforementioned elevated photo-stability and low pH-dependence, together with the presence of numerous oxidized groups on the CND surface, are encouraging points for the application of these nanoparticles as platform for the detection of metal ion. In fact, according to literature, the surface functional groups of CNDs can interact with metal ions inducing a change in fluorescence intensity [85]. For these reasons, we tested the efficiency of the CNDs produced from OSWs as sensors for cations detection.

Firstly, we have examined the fluorescence quenching of the CNDs in the presence of different cations, such as Fe3+, Fe2+, Na+, Ca+, Mg2+, K+, Ag+, NH4 +, Mn2+, Pd2+, Mo4+ and Cu2+. In the absence of metal ions, the fluorescence emission intensity of the synthesized CDs is significantly high. Upon spiking with the cations, variations in the fluorescence intensities are observed (Fig. 5 a). Fe3+ results to be the most efficient quencher for our CNDs. In fact, 200 μM of this cation induces a quenching in the emission spectra equal to the 90% of the original intensity. On the other hand, the spiking with the same concentration of other metal ions exhibits low or negligible quenching in the fluorescence emission spectrum. We have to point out that some ions such as Fe2+, Mn2+, Pd2+ and Cu2+ can induce a decrease as well in fluorescence intensity (around 50% of the original fluorescence value), even if not comparable to the quenching caused by Fe3+ ions. Besides the selectivity test, interfering experiments have been conducted. The CNDs have been spiked with solutions of 200 μM of Fe3+ and 50 μM of different cations. As shown in Fig. 5b, no substantial changes in the fluorescence quenching is observed in presence of other cations. Therefore, these results demonstrate that the produced CNDs are selective towards Fe3+, even in metal ion mixtures. The high response to Fe3+ can be ascribed to its coordination and binding affinity with the oxygenated groups on the surface of CNDs [70,86–88].

Subsequently, the sensitivity of the CND sensor to Fe3+ ions has been analyzed. The fluorescence response of CNDs to different concentrations of Fe3+ has been monitored in the range of 0–350 μM. A gradual decrease in the PL intensity is observed with increasing Fe3+ concentrations allowing the quantification of Fe3+ ions (Fig. 5c). The plot of quenching efficiency (F0-F/F0) versus Fe3+ concentration displays a good linearity in the range of 0–50 μM (Fig. 5d), where F0 and F are the PL intensities of CNDs at 450 nm in the absence and presence of Fe3+, respectively. The inset in Fig. 5d shows the calibration curve fitted linearly (R2 = 0.9985). Limit of detection (LOD) has been estimated to be 1 μM and the sensitivity of the probe has been calculated to be 0.0133 μM−1 (the equations applied to calculate sensitivity, LOD and LOQ are reported in SI) [89].

To demonstrate the applicability of the CND probes for the detection of Fe3+ in real samples, the effect of the interference of tap-water has been evaluated and compared to a synthetic solution obtained by mixing different ions present in water (Ca+, Mg2+, Na+, K+). The resulting interference is similar in the two media, showing a slight difference in the quenching efficiency compared to Ultrapure water (Fig. 5b). We propose that this difference arises from the presence of other cations that are changing the ionic strength of the media. On the base of this result, the fluorescence calibration of the probe to the [Fe3+] has been repeated on tap water. The probe demonstrates to well tolerate the effect of ionic forces of existed salt ions in the water, even if the performances of the sensor resulted slightly different in this more complex media. The calibration line in the range 0–50 μM shows a good linearity, with a sensitivity of 0.0055 μM−1 and a LOD 1.4 μM (Fig. 5e).

The performance of the CND probe in Fe3+detection has been assessed as well in real water samples: tap water, river water and drinking water. Prior to analysis the real samples have been subjected to centrifugation and filtration, to avoid the adverse effect of large solid particles. The water samples have been spiked with diverse concentrations of Fe3+ solution, then the fluorescence intensity has been recorded and Fe3+ concentration calculated based on the obtained linear formula and the calibration curve (Fig. 5e). The results, summarized in Table S2, show good recoveries (comprised between 97.7 and 103.2) and high analytical precision (with %RSD from 0.49 to 4.81, n = 3).

It is interesting to notice that the LODs found for this probe are lower than the maximum permissible limit (5.36 μM) reported by World Health Organization (WHO). This foresees possible applications of the CNDs in the quantification of Fe content in drinkable and environmental water pools [40]. Furthermore, the range of linearity of this probe is well centered in the range of concentration of Fe in human plasma (18 μM) and mammalian cells (total cell Fe concentration = 20–100 μM, cellular labile Fe pool = 5 μM), prompting a potential application for the detection of biological Fe [90,91].

Understanding the photoluminescence quenching mechanism of the CNDs is fundamental in order to exploit these nanoparticles at the maximum of their possibilities, allowing to identify the most appropriate detection strategy and design the best performing sensor [92]. The quenching mechanism can vary from case to case depending on the composition of the carbon dots, their surface functionalization and the nature of the quencher/analyte. Seven different mechanisms are known for CND quenching [93]. Among these, the two most common are the collisional/dynamic quenching and the static quenching. Dynamic quenching can take place when the CNDs pass from the excited state to the ground one, by the collision with the quencher, due to energy or charge transfer. Instead, static quenching occurs when a non-fluorescent ground-state complex is formed through the interaction between CDs and quencher, preventing the emission of fluorescent radiation [93]. We have started our study of the quenching mechanism of CNDs by Fe3+ ions by applying the Stern Volmer equation:

F
0 and F refer to the fluorescence intensities of CNDs in the absence and presence of Fe3+ ions respectively; K
sv and k
q represent the Stern-Volmer and bimolecular quenching constants respectively, [Q] is the concentration of the quencher which is here Fe3+, τ0 is the average fluorescence lifetime of CNDs in the absence of the quencher.

This equation allows to distinguish if the quenching mechanism is purely static or dynamic (resulting in linear correlation), or if it is derived from the combination of different mechanisms (showing a deviation at higher concentration). The Stern-Volmer equation in our case shows a linear correlation ruling out the possibility of static-dynamic combined quenching mechanism [94]. As illustrated in Fig. 6 a, the plot of F
0
/F versus [Fe3+] shows a good linear correlation (R2 = 0.992) with a Stern-Volmer quenching constant K
sv equal to be 0.0316 μM−1 (3.16 × 104 M−1). To clearly distinguish between dynamic and static quenching mechanisms, fluorescence lifetime measurements have been conducted for CNDs. As illustrated in Fig. 6b. The lifetime does not change in the presence or in the absence of Fe3+ (τ0/τ ≈ 1), indicating that the mechanism is static [93]. Furthermore, bimolecular quenching constant k
q can be calculated from K
sv and τ, found to be 9 × 1013 M−1s−1, which is higher than the diffusion-controlled limit (1010 M−1s−1). This also supports the static quenching mechanism resulted by ground-state complex formation between the surface functional groups of CNDs and Fe3+ ions [76]. Even if the fluorescence lifetime is the main discerning parameter between dynamic and static quenching, a series of other experiments can be performed to further investigate the quenching mechanism. Frequently, for static quenching, the formation of the ground-state complex can result in the change of the absorption spectrum of the CDs [93]. This is true in our case, where after the interaction with Fe3+, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CNDs shows a higher absorption in the range of 260–400 nm (Fig. 6d). On other hand, if a Fe chelating agent, such as EDTA, is added to the Fe3+ quenched CNDs, the fluorescence is restored as shown in Fig. S7a. This is because EDTA competes with CNDs in forming complexes with Fe3+ and sequestering Fe from the carbon dots. These are further proofs that a non-fluorescent ground-state complex is formed between the cation and the dots, and the mechanism can be classified as static quenching [95]. To further investigate the nature of the CND-Fe complex we have acquired FTIR spectra of CNDs in presence of Fe3+ (Fig. 6c). Evident changes in intensity of the peaks at 1030 cm−1 (C-OH), 1397 cm−1 (COO−) and 3380 cm−1 (O-H) can be detected. Furthermore, the peak at 1652 cm−1, attributed to carbonyl groups, presents a slight wavenumber shift. These findings indicate that the CNDs interact with Fe ions through the carboxylic and hydroxyl groups present on the surface of the carbon dots [96].

There is still a possibility that the quenching could derive from an aggregation phenomenon among nanoparticles, induced by the complex formation. In order to rule out this scenario, we performed a fluorescence correlation spectroscopy (FCS) study. This technique is able to determine the diffusion times of fluorescent diffusing species in solution. At the same time, it is possible to quantify the number of fluorescent species present in the confocal volume. From the obtained correlograms, it is evident that no difference in diffusion time and hence hydrodynamic radius can be detected between CNDs in water and CNDs in 30 and 100 μM solution of Fe3+ (Fig. S7b). This demonstrates that no aggregation takes place in presence of the Fe cations [97]. It is interesting to observe that, at lag time zero, the value of the correlation function (G0) is increasing at higher concentrations of Fe3+ (Fig. S7b). G0 is inversely proportional to the number of fluorescent emitting species present in the confocal volume (Equation S(4)). Even if the CND concentration in the three samples is the same, we can observe different concentrations of fluorescent species, which are inversely proportional to the Fe 3+ amount. Accordingly, the average fluorescent count rate is decreasing with increasing concentrations of Fe3+ (Fig. S7c). This is a further proof of the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between CNDs and Fe3+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ion sensing with CNDs-EDA and CNDs-Dopa
ce:section.ce:para: Based on our findings regarding the quenching mechanism of CNDs with Fe3+, we thought that tuning the surface chemistry of the nanoparticles could modulate the affinity of our CNDs for Fe3+, by creating thus complexes with improved or reduced stability with the analyte. In particular, CNDs-EDA should intercalate amino groups to the oxidized moieties, presenting a reduced amount of carboxylic and hydroxyl groups on the surface. We speculate that, increasing the number of amino groups and decreasing the oxidized ones, could reduce the affinity of CNDs to the analyte. Instead CNDs-Dopa should present stronger Fe complexing properties due to the high affinity of catechol groups for this cation.

In order to prove our hypothesis ion sensing performances of CNDs-EDA and CNDs-Dopa have been evaluated. As done for CNDs, the sensitivity of the probes has been inspected by measuring the fluorescence response to Fe3+ ions in the range of 0–350 μM and 0-600 μM for CNDs-EDA and CNDs-Dopa probes respectively (Fig. 7 a, b; Fig. S8; Fig. S9). For both probes, a decrease in fluorescence intensity is noticed while increasing the Fe3+ concentrations, allowing the sensing of this ion.

Remarkably, the modification of the CND surface fine-tunes the sensing properties of CNDs. The introduction of dopamine groups on the surface on the C-dots allows a seven-fold increase of the sensitivity of the probe (0.0388 μM−1) and reduction of the LOD to 0.32 μM (Fig. 7c and d). This confirms our hypothesis that CNDs-Dopa can create more stable state ground complexes with Fe, improving the quenching efficiency of the dots. On the other side, the introduction of ethylenediamine in the CND structure is resulting in an increased nitrogen and reduced oxygen content. The change in the CND composition, and hence surface, is clear from XPS data. The elemental composition is changing from 58.5 (C at%) 37.6 (O at%) 3.9 (N at%) for CNDs to 73,4 (C at%) 18,8 (O at%) 17.8 (N at%) for CNDs-EDA. Considering that the diameter of CNDs and CNDs-EDA are similar (respectively 2.8 ± 0.6 and 2.5 ± 0.7), we can conclude that the surface density of azo-groups is increasing while the density of OH and COOH groups is decreasing. The reduced density of oxidized groups on the CND surface affects their affinity for Fe3+ [54], reducing sensitivity (0.00201 μM−1) and increasing the LOD of the probe (11 μM, Fig. 7c and d). As a result of our surface functionalization, we obtained three probes with increasing sensitivity in the following order: CNDs-EDA < CNDs < CNDs-Dopa. Notwithstanding the loss in sensitivity, CNDs-EDA present a much wider linear range, between 0 and 200 μM than the other probes (0-50 μM for CNDs and 0-5 μM for CNDs-Dopa). The quenching mechanism has been investigated as well for the surface modified CNDs. The fluorescence life time of these dots does not vary in presence of the quencher, confirming the static quenching mechanism (Fig. S10). The Fe3+ sensing parameters for the obtained probes, CNDs, CNDs-EDA and CNDs-Dopa, are competitive among other sensing probes obtained both from chemical reagents or natural sources (Table S3) [35,76,92,98–110]. The strong point of CNDs-Dopa is the extremely high sensitivity in the 0-5 μM range (0.0388 μM−1), that is among the highest reported for CND based probes. Furthermore, CNDs-Dopa, with their sub-micromolar LOD, places above the average of the reported sensor. In fact, the Fe3+ CND based probes in literature have LODs that spaces in the nM-μM range, with just few CNDs with LOD in the nM order [111–113]. On the other side CNDs-EDA presents a linear range comparable with widest present in literature. It also important to consider that, differently from our CNDs, none of the best performing CNDs has been obtained by wastes or requalified side-product. With the reported in this work, we demonstrate that the sensing abilities of CNDs can be tailored toward higher selectivity or wider linear range, obtaining C-dot suitable for a wide range of applications.

The functionalization of CNDs with dopamine succeeded in improving the sensitivity of the probe for Fe3+, but did not increase its specificity (Fig. S11). Also, while employing CNDs-Dopa as a probe, some metal ions such as Fe2+, Cu2+ and Mn2+ were still competing with Fe3+ in the fluorescence quenching. To improve the selectivity of the probe toward Fe3+, we decided to incorporate the CNDs inside an ion imprinted polymer (IIP) [114,115], where selective pockets to Fe3+ have been created during the polymerization using FeCl3 as a template. The design of this polymer has been inspired by metalloproteins, where the metallic cation is hosted in a pocket and coordinated by the exposed groups of the protein [116,117]. Analogously, in the IIP, the cations can be coordinated by hydroxyl groups in pockets inside the polymer [118]. The IIP have been prepared through a sol-gel process starting with the functionalized CNDs with APTES on the surface (Fig. 8 a).

We speculate that APTES can be absorbed on the CNDs by electrostatic interactions, or can react with the epoxide group by nucleophilic ring opening [119]. Once functionalized with siloxanes, which act as starting point for the polymerization, the CNDs have been blended with the other components of the polymer: the monomer (TEOS), the template (FeCl3) and the initiator (NH4OH). It is important to specify that, during the CND derivatization, silanization side reactions could occur between APTES and the hydroxyl and carbonyl groups of the CNDs, interfering with the polymerization reaction. Nevertheless, the TEM images shows well uniform and well-formed silica microspheres, proving the success of the synthesis (Fig. 8b). Non imprinted polymer (NIP) has been prepared as control using the same procedure, but without the addition of the template. The prepared IIP and NIP have been characterized by TEM and SEM to study their size and morphology, as illustrated in Fig. 8 b, c and Fig. S12. They exhibit a particle diameter in the range of 300–500 nm with a clear porous structure of IIP compared to NIP.
The fluorescence spectra of IIP and NIP have been acquired after the Fe3+ removal ions showing an emission peak at 437 and 450 nm respectively (Fig. 9 a, Ex: 360 nm).

To study the sensitivity of IIP, the fluorescence intensity of IIP and NIP have been investigated at different Fe3+ concentrations (2.5, 5, 10, 30, 50, 100 and 200 μM, Fig. 9b). Both IIP and NIP show a fluorescence quenching with increasing Fe3+ concentration. Nevertheless, IIP probe results more efficient compared to NIP, showing a higher sensitivity at lower Fe3+ concentrations. Instead, NIP response to Fe3+ ions starts at relatively higher concentration (i.e. 10 μM). This behavior can be ascribed to the Fe3+ specific cavities within the IIP [120]. Such cavities, together with the microsphere porosity allows the quencher to reach easily the CNDs (Fig. 8b and 8c), quenching them when in their proximity.

Furthermore, the selectivity, which is the main role behind this developed IIP, has been investigated and compared with other probes, such as CNDs and CNDs-Dopamine. The cations that showed the highest quenching efficiency with CNDs (Fe3+, Fe2+, Cu2+ and Mn2+) were selected as targets of this study. Upon the formation of the IIP around the C-dots, a strong improvement in the selectivity is recorded. CNDs-IIP microspheres show a complete selectivity for Fe3+ at 25 μM concentration (Fig. 9c). The presence of the same concentration of other cations does not induce a quenching of the fluorescence. It can be concluded that the cavities imprinted in the polymer allow the exclusive recombination with Fe (III).
Olive solid wastes were obtained from an olive oil extraction plant in Bari (Italy). All chemicals and reagents were purchased form Sigma-Aldrich and used without further purification.

Steady state fluorescence spectral measurements were conducted in an emission range of 350–650 nm using Perkin Elmer LS55 Fluorescence spectrophotometer, the spectra were acquired at different excitation wavelengths from 300 to 480 nm with increments of 20 nm and a slit open of 2 nm. UV–Vis spectra were recorded on Beckman Coulter DU 800 Spectrophotometer from 200 to 800 nm. Fluorescence decay analysis was performed with Horiba Jobin Yvon fluorimeter using a TSPC controller and a fixed wavelength Nano-LEDs 375 nm excitation source. CDs size and morphology were studied by an atomic force (AFM) and transmission electron (TEM) microscopies; AFM images and profiles were measured by multimode V AFM from Bruker microscope, the AFM samples were prepared by drop casting diluted CDs solution on mica substrates, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were acquired by JEOL JEM 1400-Plus microscope operated at an accelerating voltage of 120 kV, equipped with GATAN US1000 CCD Camera; samples were prepared by drop casting diluted CDs solution on Lacey carbon film coated copper TEM grids. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out with SPECS SAGE HR 100 system equipped with 100 mm mean radius PHOIBOS analyzer where MgKα X-ray source was used, for XPS samples, CDs solutions were drop casted on titanium-glass coated substrates and left dried under vacuum for 24 h. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded with Thermo scientific Nicolet 6700 FTIR in reflection mode using SmartArc FTIR accessory equipped with ZnS window, the test was performed by adding a drop of targeted CDs solution on the ZnS window and left to dry at room temperature. Z-potential measurements were conducted using a Malvern Nano ZS90 Zetasizer. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images were recorded with JEOL JSM-6490LV instrument, equipped with SE and BE detectors and OXFORD INCA EDXS system. FCS was performed with Zeiss LSM 880 confocal microscope controlled by Zen black software. GaASP and PMTr detectors for single fluorescence molecules detection and dynamic characterization were used. Measurements were performed with Zeiss C-Apochromat 40x (N.A. 1.2) water immersion objective excitation with excitation wavelength of 405 nm. Fluorescence emission was detected in the range of 420–600 nm. NuncTM Lab-Tek Q5 Chambered Coverglass (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) were used as support for the sample. QuickFit 3.044 free software was employed for FCS data analysis. The data were fitted with single component 3D normal diffusion model. The confocal volume was determined using 50 nM Atto 488 solution.

OSWs were purified from residual oil and impurities by mean of soxhlet extraction. OSWs were pyrolyzed into COSWs at 600 °C for 1hr [51]. After cooling to room temperature COSWs were mechanically ground into fine powder. 100 mg of the obtained sample was dispersed and sonicated for 10 min in 10 ml of 0.45 wt% H2O2 solution and refluxed for 90 min. CNDs were separated from larger carbon particles by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 20 min. The supernatant was collected and filtrated over 0.22 μm microfilter. Finally, the CNDs were purified by dialysis against Ultrapure water for 24 h (cut-off 500-1000 Da). When required the CNDs were lyophilized to obtain a dry brown powder.

For the synthesis of CNDs-EDA 100 mg of COSWs were mixed with 200 mg of ethylenediamine and dispersed by sonication in 10 ml solution of 0.45 wt% H2O2. For the synthesis of CNDs-Dopa 100 mg of COSWs were mixed with 100 mg of dopamine and dispersed by sonication in 10 ml solution of 0.45 wt% H2O2. CNDs-EDA and CNDs-Dopa were prepared and purified following the procedure previously described for CNDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for Fe3+ sensing with CNDs, CNDs-EDA and CNDs-Dopa
ce:section.ce:para: 300 μM metal ion solutions (Fe3+, Fe2+, Na+, Ca+, Mg2+, K+, Ag+, NH4 +, Mn2+, Pd2+, Mo4+, Cu2+ and mixed ions) were prepared in Ultrapure water using their respective salts (FeCl3, FeCl2, NaCl, CaCl2, MgCl2.H2O, K2CO3, AgNO3, NH4Cl, MnCl2, Pd(OCOCH3)2, MoS2, and CuSO4).

For the evaluation of the selectivity, 0.5 ml of CNDs solution (40 μg ml−1) were added to 1 ml of Ultrapure water and the fluorescence intensity at λem = 450 nm (λex = 360 nm) was recorded as F0. Analogously, 0.5 ml of CNDs solution were added to 1 ml of different metal ion solutions producing solutions with final concentrations of 200 μM of ions, and 13 μg ml−1 of CNDs. Samples were first vortexed and then left incubated statically for 5 min. The fluorescence intensity was measured and recorded as F.
Interfering of metal ions with Fe3+ was studied by measuring the fluorescence of CNDs solutions incubated with 200 μM of Fe3+ and 50 μM of competitive metal ions.

For quantitative determination of Fe3+ similar procedure was followed; 0.5 ml CNDs solution was added to 1 ml of Fe3+ salt solutions having varied concentrations to get Fe3+ concentration of (0, 2.5, 5, 7.5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 40, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 and 350 μM), this procedure was followed also for CNDs-EDA with [Fe3+] of (0, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 and 350 μM) and CNDs-Dopa having [Fe3+] of (0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 10, 25, 50, 100, 150, 200, 300, 400, 500 and 600 μM).

2 ml of CNDs (1 mg/ml) solution were mixed in a round bottom flask with 10 ml ethanol, and 80 μl aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES). The reaction mixture was stirred vigorously for 2 h at room temperature [121]. 200 μl of ammonia hydroxide solution (25%) and 22 mg FeCl3 were added to the above solution. After 5 min of stirring, 100 μl tetraethoxysilane (TEOS) dissolved in 10 ml ethanol were added dropwise. The reaction mixture was stirred at RT for 24 h. CNDs-IIP was collected by centrifugation at 1000 rpm for 15 min and washed 3 times with tetrahydrofuran (THF) 3 times with 1 mM EDTA in Ultrapure water and 3 times in Ultrapure water to remove the Fe3+ template. Non imprinted polymers (NIPs) were prepared similarly, without adding the Fe3+ template.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Procedure for Fe3+ sensing with CNDs-IIP and CND-NIP
ce:section.ce:para: For quantitative determination of Fe3+ 0.5 ml of CNDs-IIP and CNDs-NIP solutions (200 μg ml−1) were added to 1 ml of Fe3+ salt solutions at various concentrations (0, 4, 20, 40, 60, 100, 200, 300, 600 μM). Samples were first vortexed and then left incubated statically for 5 min. The fluorescence intensity was recorded.

In conclusion, in this work we present a novel strategy for the synthesis of fluorescent CNDs from olive solid wastes. OSWs are convenient precursors for the CND synthesis as they are abundant, cheap and problematic under the waste management aspect. Furthermore, we demonstrate that we can modulate the sensing properties of the produced CNDs toward the detection of Fe3+. By modulating the surface chemistry of the CNDs, we can tune the affinity of the particles toward the cation, and hence the stability of the non-fluorescent ground-state complex formed between the dots and Fe3+. In particular, dopamine doping enhances the stability of the complex, improving the sensitivity of the probe. On the other side, the introduction of amino moieties in the CNDs structure reduces the affinity with the analyte, leading to reduced sensitivity but wider linear range. The quenching mechanism has been proved to be static and several proofs of the formation of a non-fluorescent ground state complex between CNDs and Fe3+ have been provided in this work. Furthermore, we have improved the selectivity of the probe by growing Fe3+ template IIP around the CNDs. This work clearly demonstrates that, even if as prepared CNDs already presents intrinsic sensing abilities, it is fundamental to engineer the nanoparticles to improve the probe sensing abilities, allowing their effective application in the sensing field.
